Dear Msi
Kutchins:
The Senate Health
and Welfare Committee intends
to hold a
hearing on Proposition Fiver
The purpose of this hearing is to
educate the public as
to the policy considerations of the said
proposition.
It is the intention of the Committee staff,
as
well as
the members of the Committee,
to conduct the hearing in
a non—advisory, impartial manner.
Essentially, every effort will
be maﬂe to give both the opponents and proponents of the propo—
sition equal time to explain their position.
It is antiCipated, however,
that various members of the
Committee may be asked for their personal opinions concerning the
proposition.
In fact, there may be members of the Committee who
openly Show their particular bias either for or against the
prnposition.
Please advise us
as
to the potential civil and/or
Criminal liability to staff members who participate in prepara-
tion for this hearingr
Needless
to say,
the staff will perform
the typical functions,
such as preparing,
typing,
and releasing
information to the press,
contacting witnesses, writing a back-
gnund report,
and preparing questions for the members of the
Committeei
I am of the opinion that staff would not be subject
to any civil and/or criminal liability for performing basic
legislative functions relative to informing the public
as
to
the potential public policy effect of a proposed ballot measure.

practices Commission.
Thank you for your assistance in this matterl

STEVE HERUM

sections.question: You have specifically inquired as to any potential civil and/or criminal liability staff members may have as a result of preparing for this hearing.
sections.conclusion: From the facts you have provided us, it is the opinion of the staff that the hearings which the Senate Health and Welfare Committee plans to hold would not result in activity reportable under the Political Reform Act. The provision of a forum for both sides of a political issue to express their opinions is not activity resulting in reportable campaign activity.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether staff members of the Senate Health and Welfare Committee would incur civil and/or criminal liability under the Political Reform Act for preparing and conducting a public hearing on Proposition Five designed to educate the public on policy considerations with balanced representation from both proponents and opponents.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC staff concluded that the planned Senate Health and Welfare Committee hearing would not generate reportable campaign activity under the Political Reform Act because providing a balanced forum for both sides to express opinions on a proposition is not reportable campaign activity. The Commission has no jurisdiction over violations of statutes regulating use of state funds, so such questions should be directed to the Attorney General.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (dated August 30, 1975) and the FPPC response letter (dated September 15, 1978). Date discrepancy noted: letter header shows September 15, 1972 but document ID indicates 1978. OCR quality issues with some text degradation. Incoming request signed by Steve Herum, response signed by Barbara Campbell, FPPC Counsel.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have specifically inquired as to any potential civil and/or criminal liability staff members may have as a result of preparing for this hearing.

CONCLUSION: From the facts you have provided us, it is the opinion of the staff that the hearings which the Senate Health and Welfare Committee plans to hold would not result in activity reportable under the Political Reform Act. The provision of a forum for both sides of a political issue to express their opinions is not activity resulting in reportable campaign activity.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter responding to an inquiry from Senator John Garamendi's office regarding potential liability for legislative staff conducting a public hearing on Proposition Five. The FPPC concluded that such hearings would not constitute reportable campaign activity under the Political Reform Act.
